title: Seasoned wood: a primer
subtitle: Moisture content, species properties, and how to identify well-seasoned stock
excerpt: Seasoned wood is the starting point for most carved household objects: stable, consistent, and workable with hand tools across a range of hardness. This primer covers what seasoning means, how to identify kiln-dried and air-dried stock, which species suits which project, and how to store timber before use.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for seasoning and species working properties; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for moisture content and drying method.

glossaryTerms.definition: Timber stabilised through drying to 8 to 12 percent moisture content for indoor use. At this moisture content, wood is dimensionally stable and predictable under tools.
Timber dried in a controlled heat chamber to a specified moisture content; faster than air-drying and producing consistent results, but with higher residual stress than slowly air-dried wood.

Timber stacked and dried naturally in a covered, ventilated space. The rule of thumb is one year of drying per 25 mm of thickness. Slower than kiln-drying but produces wood with lower internal stress.

content.text: The difference between a spoon that cracks in the drawer after 3 months and one that lasts for decades is usually the starting material.

; timber dried to 8 to 12 percent moisture content; is dimensionally stable. It does not shrink, check, or crack when brought into a heated room. Green or partially dried wood does all three. Knowing how to identify and select well-seasoned stock is the first skill in any wood carving or joinery project.
Most timber sold by builders merchants and hardwood suppliers is either

. Kiln-dried timber is the standard from larger merchants: dried quickly and consistently to 10 to 12 percent moisture. It is reliable for most carving and joinery projects but can be slightly more brittle than air-dried stock of the same species; the rapid drying process introduces stress that careful slow drying avoids. Air-dried timber from a small specialist supplier or a local sawmill has lower internal stress and often shows more character in the figure, but moisture content can vary and should be checked with a moisture meter before use. For most hand-tool carving projects, either method produces equivalent results.

Checks at the end grain are the most reliable sign of correctly seasoned timber: small radial cracks (checks) running out from the centre of the end grain are normal and do not indicate a structural problem. Checks that run more than a quarter of the radius deep can affect carved pieces with thin walls or narrow handles. Avoid stock with visible blue-grey staining; this indicates fungal activity at too-high moisture content. Cupping (the face bowing across the width) and bowing along the length indicate that drying was uneven; these pieces will continue to move.

Sycamore: the benchmark for small treen; pale, close-grained, food-safe with an oil finish. Carves easily and takes a fine surface. The first species to try for spoons, spatulas, and boards. Beech: harder than sycamore, heavier, equally pale. Better for bowls and items that will take sustained load. Cherry: warm red-brown, fine-grained, less hard than beech. Takes oil well and deepens in colour with age. Walnut: dark, dense, and visually striking. Reacts with iron tools to produce a surface stain that cleans off with a citric acid wipe. Oak: very hard and heavy; the best seasoned species for structural items (bookends, frames, tool handles) but demanding on edge tools. Not ideal for spoon carving. Ash: pale, ring-porous grain pattern, resilient. The traditional British handle wood; also works for spoons and household objects.

Store purchased timber horizontally in the room where it will be used for at least 2 weeks before starting work. Timber that has been stored in a cold, damp shed will re-absorb moisture from the atmosphere; bringing it indoors allows the moisture content to equalise to the room's conditions. Sticker the boards (stack them with thin spacers between each layer) so air can circulate around every face. Timber stored in a heated room below 8 percent moisture content can be too dry for fine carving; the surface will tear rather than cut cleanly. A moisture content between 8 and 12 percent is the working range for most seasoned-wood carving and joinery.
